title: Tokens
lesson:
A large language model never actually sees your words. Before any text reaches the model, it is chopped into small pieces called tokens. A token is the basic unit a model reads and writes — not quite a letter, not quite a word, usually somewhere in between.

Everything an LLM does — from reading your question to writing its answer — happens one token at a time.

The supermarket-scanner analogy

Picture a supermarket checkout. The cashier can't "understand" a tin of beans by looking at it. They scan the barcode, and the till looks up a number in its database. The product becomes an ID.

Tokens work the same way. The text beans might become token ID 3243. The model only ever works with these ID numbers; the words are just labels we humans read.

So when people say a model "predicts the next word", what really happens is it predicts the next token ID from a fixed list called the vocabulary (the full set of tokens a model knows, often around 100,000 to 200,000 entries).

Tokens are not words

This is the part beginners get wrong, so let's be concrete:

  • Common short words like the or cat are usually one token each.
  • Longer or rarer words get split. The word tokenization might become token + ization.
  • A made-up word like flibbertigibbet could become five or six pieces.
  • Spaces matter: " dog" (with a leading space) and "dog" are often different tokens.
  • Even a single emoji can cost several tokens.

The exact splitting rules are the job of a Tokenization step, which we cover next — here we just care about the unit itself.

💡 A handy rule of thumb for English: 1 token ≈ 4 characters ≈ 0.75 words. So 1,000 tokens is roughly 750 words, about a page and a half. This is a ballpark, not a law — code, numbers, and other languages can be far less efficient.

Why you should care: money and limits

Tokens are the currency of AI. Two huge practical things are measured in tokens, not words:

  • Cost. When you call a hosted model's API (a paid online service), you pay per token — both the tokens you send (input) and the tokens it generates (output). Output is usually pricier. See Cost-per-Token Analysis.
  • Memory limit. A model can only hold so many tokens at once — its Context Windows. Think of a desk with a fixed size: once it's covered in paper, something has to come off before more goes on. That "desk" is measured in tokens.

The conveyor-belt analogy

Imagine reading a sentence through a narrow slot, one tile at a time, and you must guess the next tile before it appears. That is exactly how a model generates text.

It reads all the tokens so far, predicts the single most likely next token, adds it to the line, then repeats. This one-at-a-time loop is called autoregression (using your own previous outputs as the next input).

It's why longer answers take longer and cost more: every extra token is another full lap around the belt. For the bigger picture, see How AI Models Work.

Counting tokens in code

You rarely count tokens by hand. Each model family ships a tokenizer — the tool that turns text into tokens and back. Here is the common OpenAI library, tiktoken:

import tiktoken

enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")  # a real vocabulary
text = "Tokens are the currency of AI."
ids = enc.encode(text)        # text -> list of token IDs
print(len(ids), ids)          # e.g. 7 [25503, 527, 279, ...]
print(enc.decode(ids))        # IDs -> back to the text

Notice the count: a six-word sentence became about seven tokens, because the trailing period and some word-splits add up. Different makers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Meta) use different tokenizers, so the same sentence can have slightly different counts on each platform.

Where this bites in real products

Say you build a chatbot over a 50-page PDF. You can't just paste all 50 pages in — that might be 30,000 tokens and blow past the context window, or cost more than you'd like per question. So you measure token counts, trim, and decide what to include.

Tokens are also why streaming feels word-by-word: the model emits tokens as they're produced. One last distinction: tokens are the input/output unit, while Embeddings are the meaning-as-numbers the model builds from those tokens internally — related, but not the same. For the absolute basics, revisit LLM Basics.
mentalModel: Tokens are LEGO bricks of text: the model never builds with whole sentences, only with these small, pre-made pieces — and you pay by the brick, both to read and to build.
takeaways: A token is a sub-word chunk, not a whole word.
You pay per token — both input and output.

diagram.caption: A single word is split into sub-word tokens, each mapped to an ID — and you are billed per token, not per word.

quiz.q: Roughly how many tokens is a 600-word English email?
quiz.explain: Using the rule 1 token ≈ 0.75 words, divide words by 0.75: 600 / 0.75 ≈ 800 tokens. Token counts are higher than word counts because words often split into pieces.

quiz.q: Why might the exact same sentence count as a different number of tokens on OpenAI versus Anthropic?
options: Anthropic charges more, so it counts extra tokens
Each provider uses a different tokenizer with a different vocabulary
Sentences are random, so counts are never stable
One counts words and the other counts characters
quiz.explain: Token counts come from the tokenizer, and each model family ships its own with a different vocabulary and splitting rules. Always count with the tokenizer for the model you're actually calling.

quiz.q: A user sends a 20-token prompt and the model writes a 500-token answer. Which usually drives most of the cost?
options: The 20 input tokens, because input is always pricier
The 500 output tokens, since there are far more of them and output is often priced higher
Neither — only the first token is billed
It's a flat fee, so token counts don't matter
quiz.explain: You pay for both input and output, and here output is 25x larger and typically priced higher per token. Long, chatty answers quietly dominate the bill.

quiz.q: Which input is likely to be the LEAST token-efficient (most tokens per idea)?
options: A block of source code with symbols, or non-English text
A list of single-digit numbers spelled in English
quiz.explain: Code symbols, long numbers, emoji, and many non-English scripts lack single dedicated tokens, so the tokenizer glues together many small fragments — costing far more tokens per idea than plain English.

mistakes: Thinking 1 token = 1 word. Tokens are usually sub-word chunks. A safe ballpark for English is roughly 4 characters per token, so always estimate, never assume.
Forgetting that output tokens cost money too — and often cost more than input. A chatty model that pads every answer can quietly double your bill even when your prompts are short.

Assuming token counts are the same across providers. OpenAI, Anthropic, and Meta use different tokenizers, so the identical text can tokenize to different counts. Always count with the tokenizer for the model you're actually calling.

Ignoring non-English text and code. Languages like Japanese or Hindi, plus source code, emoji, and long numbers, are far less token-efficient — the same idea can cost 2–3x more tokens than English prose.
Pasting in giant documents blindly. A long PDF can exceed the context window or get silently truncated, so the model never even sees the part you cared about.
Confusing tokens with characters when setting limits. A max_tokens setting of 100 is not 100 letters or 100 words — it's about 75 words, and a too-low value will chop the answer off mid-sentence.
exercise:
Install tiktoken (or use any online token counter) and tokenize four strings: (1) the plain word hello, (2) a long rare word like antidisestablishmentarianism, (3) a line of code such as for i in range(10):, and (4) one emoji. Record the token count for each. Then write one sentence explaining why the rare word and the emoji cost more tokens than hello.

Stretch goal: Take a 300-word block of text, count its tokens, and compute the real ratio of tokens-to-words. Compare it to the 0.75-words-per-token rule of thumb, then estimate what 1,000 such requests would cost if input is priced at roughly $0.50 per million tokens.
solution: Expect something like: hello = 1 token; antidisestablishmentarianism = ~6–8 tokens (it gets split into many sub-word pieces because it's rare); for i in range(10): = ~7–9 tokens (punctuation and symbols each take a slot); a single emoji = often 2–3 tokens (stored as several underlying bytes). Rare words and emoji cost more because the tokenizer has no single ID for them, so it falls back to gluing together smaller fragments. For the stretch goal, 300 words is roughly 400 tokens, giving ~0.75 words/token — close to the rule. 1,000 requests ≈ 400,000 tokens ≈ 0.4 million, so about $0.20 of input cost (plus output, which you'd add separately).
summary: A token is the small sub-word unit an LLM actually reads and writes; words get split into one or more tokens. Tokens are the unit of both cost (you pay per input and output token) and memory (the context window is sized in tokens). Roughly 1 token ≈ 4 characters ≈ 0.75 English words, but counts vary by language, code, and provider — so measure with the right tokenizer.

keyTerms.def: The basic chunk of text a model reads and generates — usually a sub-word piece, not a whole word or single letter.
The tool that converts text into token IDs and back. Each model family has its own, so counts differ between providers.
The full fixed list of tokens a model knows — often around 100,000–200,000 entries. The model can only use tokens from this list.
The number assigned to each token. The model works entirely with these IDs; the readable words are just labels for us.
Input tokens are what you send (your prompt); output tokens are what the model writes back. APIs charge for both, usually pricing output higher.
Generating text one token at a time, where each new token is predicted from all the tokens produced so far.
A setting that caps how many tokens the model may generate in its reply. Set it too low and the answer is cut off mid-thought.
How many tokens a given text costs. English prose is fairly efficient; code, emoji, long numbers, and many non-English languages cost more tokens per idea.
